name: Beavercreek
tagline: Top-rated schools, no city income tax, minutes from WPAFB
overview: Beavercreek is Greene County's largest city with 46,800 residents, sitting immediately adjacent to WPAFB — making it a top choice for military families. One of only three cities in Ohio with no local income tax, a point of pride written into the city charter. The Greene Town Center brings upscale shopping and dining, while easy I-675 access connects you to Columbus and Cincinnati.
schoolsDetail: Beavercreek City Schools holds an A rating and is one of Ohio's top-performing districts. Beavercreek High School ranks in the top 100 in Ohio, and Trebein Elementary was named one of the top 100 elementary schools in the state. The district is known for strong academics, award-winning music programs, and extensive AP and Honors courses.
parksAttractions: Dominick Lofino Park features a fishing lake, walking trails, sports fields, and an amphitheater for summer concerts. The Creekside Reserve is a 140-acre nature area with trails through woods and wetlands perfect for bird watching. A segment of the Little Miami Scenic Trail runs through eastern Beavercreek, connecting to a 78-mile rail trail network. The Greene Town Center hosts free summer concerts and community events.
housingMarket: Median home price is $265,000 with steady 2–3% annual appreciation. Homes range from mid-century brick ranches to large new-construction properties in developments like Spring Meadows and River Reserve. No city income tax combined with reasonable property taxes makes Beavercreek one of the most financially attractive suburbs in the region. Homes go under contract in under a week on average.
funFacts: Beavercreek is one of only three cities in Ohio with no local income tax — written into the city charter that any income tax must be approved by voters. Residents have repeatedly voted to keep it income-tax-free, a point of community pride. Adjacent WPAFB is home to the National Museum of the U.S. Air Force, the oldest and largest aviation museum in the world — and it's free.

name: Fairborn
tagline: Adjacent to WPAFB — the #1 choice for PCS moves
overview: Home to WPAFB's Gate 12A, Fairborn is the top choice for military PCS moves — you can literally be on base in 3 minutes. A city of 34,700 with Wright State University adding college-town energy, Fairborn offers the most affordable prices of any community directly adjacent to the base. The military community is strong, and the area is well-served by restaurants, retail, and services catering to base personnel.
schoolsDetail: Fairborn City Schools is a B- rated district with 5 schools including Fairborn High School. The district has been on an upward improvement trajectory and participates in the Miami Valley Career Technology Center for vocational programs. While not as highly ranked as neighboring Beavercreek, it offers solid academics in a community deeply shaped by the military.
parksAttractions: Fairborn offers 838 acres of parkland including Chautauqua Park, Community Park, and access to the Little Miami Scenic Trail for biking and hiking. The National Museum of the U.S. Air Force on WPAFB is just minutes away — the world's largest aviation museum with over 360 aircraft on display, and it's free. Cedar Creek Valley offers natural walking paths and wildlife watching.
housingMarket: With a median home price around $180,000, Fairborn is the most affordable community directly adjacent to WPAFB — making it especially popular with E-5 through O-3 personnel maximizing BAH. Most homes are traditional ranch and split-level houses from the 1950s–1980s. The market is active with military buyers and homes typically go under contract within 2 weeks.
funFacts: Fairborn's name combines two founding communities: Fairfield and Osborn, which merged in 1950. The National Museum of the U.S. Air Force — the world's largest aviation museum — is located right on WPAFB adjacent to Fairborn and is free to the public. Wright State University, located in Fairborn, was founded specifically to serve the growing population connected to WPAFB.

name: Kettering
tagline: Dayton's most established suburb — amenities, schools, and walkability
overview: The second-largest city in Montgomery County with 57,000 residents, Kettering is Dayton's most established suburb. Named for inventor Charles Kettering, it features excellent amenities, A- rated schools, and a mature, walkable character just 10 minutes from downtown Dayton. Kettering Health Medical Center is one of the area's largest employers and anchors a strong healthcare corridor.
schoolsDetail: Kettering City Schools is an A- rated district with 12 schools. Fairmont High School is one of the top public high schools in southwest Ohio, known for a rigorous AP program and the Firebirds athletics. The district serves a diverse, engaged community and consistently scores above state averages in academic performance.
parksAttractions: Fraze Pavilion is one of Ohio's best outdoor concert venues, hosting national acts all summer long. The Kettering Recreation Complex is among the finest municipal recreation centers in the region, with pools, fitness facilities, and courts. Lincoln Park and Delco Park offer open green space, and the city's trail network connects to the broader regional path system.
housingMarket: Median price $259,000 with steady appreciation. The bulk of Kettering's homes are mid-century brick ranches and Cape Cods from the 1950s–1970s — well-built and set in mature neighborhoods with large, established trees. It's a seller's market with strong demand from buyers who want established neighborhoods over new construction. Premium pricing near the best school zones.
funFacts: Kettering is named for Charles F. Kettering, the legendary Dayton-area inventor who developed the electric starter for automobiles — one of hundreds of patents he held. The city is home to the Kettering Medical Center campus, a major regional healthcare employer. Fraze Pavilion has hosted everyone from country legends to national touring acts in its outdoor amphitheater setting.

name: Centerville
tagline: Award-winning schools, 50 parks, and Ohio's largest stone building collection
overview: Centerville is one of Dayton's most desirable suburbs, known as 'Stone City' for its remarkable collection of 100+ limestone buildings from the 19th century lining Main and Franklin Streets. With A+ schools, 50 parks connected by trails, and a growing population (+4% over 5 years), it's a top destination for families relocating to the region. Washington Township's recreation center adds exceptional community amenities.
schoolsDetail: Centerville City Schools holds an A+ rating with 13 public schools — the highest rating in the region. Centerville High School is one of Ohio's largest and most acclaimed, consistently ranked in the top tier statewide for academics, AP participation, graduation rates, and college admissions outcomes. The district is known for exceptional music, arts, and athletics programs.
parksAttractions: Centerville boasts nearly 50 parks connected by trails — one of the best park systems in southwest Ohio. Stubbs Park and Yankee Street Park are local favorites, and the Washington Township Recreation Center is a major community hub with pools, fitness facilities, and sports courts. Downtown Centerville features charming shops, Bill's Donut Shop (a local legend since 1960), and the unique limestone architecture that gives the city its nickname.
housingMarket: Median price $343,500 with strong appreciation of 8–9% in the past year. Centerville homes are characterized by traditional single-family houses in mature, established neighborhoods with 3–4 bedrooms. The typical home value is around $293,000 and rising steadily. Homes spend only 2–3 weeks on market and buyers often pay close to list price — a true seller's market.
funFacts: Centerville holds Ohio's largest collection of early stone buildings — about 100 limestone structures from the 19th century still stand, earning the city its nickname 'Stone City.' Many are located along Main and Franklin Streets, giving downtown a striking New England village character. Bill's Donut Shop has been a local institution since 1960 and draws lines every weekend morning.

name: Huber Heights
tagline: Planned brick-home community with strong family neighborhoods near WPAFB
overview: Huber Heights is a planned community of 43,000 built largely in the 1950s–70s by developer Charles H. Huber — and it holds a Guinness World Record. Located northeast of Dayton with a 13-minute drive to WPAFB, it's known for brick homes, strong family neighborhoods, the Rose Music Center, and solid affordability compared to Beavercreek just to the east.
schoolsDetail: Wayne Local Schools (Huber Heights City Schools) holds a B- rating with 8 schools. Wayne High School is known for competitive athletics and a growing STEM program. The district has been actively investing in facilities improvements and has partnerships with Wright State University for dual enrollment opportunities.
parksAttractions: The Rose Music Center at the Heights is one of Ohio's premier outdoor amphitheaters, hosting national touring acts all summer. Heathcliff Park and Marian Park offer family-friendly green space, and the Mad River Regional Trail corridor provides access for biking and walking. Huber Heights is also close to WPAFB recreational facilities available to military families.
housingMarket: Median home price $210,000 — offering strong value for northeast Dayton. Huber Heights is famous for its stock of well-built all-brick ranch and colonial homes from the 1960s–1970s, many with spacious yards. The market has seen solid appreciation with growing demand from military families and young families priced out of Beavercreek.
funFacts: Huber Heights holds the Guinness World Record for the world's largest contiguous collection of privately developed all-brick homes — over 16,000 brick homes built by Charles Huber. Developer Huber was so committed to brick construction that virtually every residential structure in the original development is brick. The Rose Music Center is consistently rated one of Ohio's best live music venues.

name: Oakwood
tagline: Dayton's most prestigious neighborhood — Ohio's #1 school district
highlights: #1 school district in the Dayton area
overview: Oakwood is Dayton's most prestigious inner suburb — a historic, walkable city of 9,400 with tree-lined streets, stunning architecture, and the region's #1-ranked school district. Its 8 square miles sit just 5 minutes from downtown Dayton, offering an unmatched character and some of the highest household incomes in Ohio. Homes here are among the most coveted and most stable in the entire state.
schoolsDetail: Oakwood City Schools is rated A+ and is consistently ranked the #1 school district in the Dayton area and among the very top in Ohio. 5 schools, all exceptional, feed into Oakwood High School — known for elite academics, championship athletics, and outstanding college admissions outcomes. The district's per-pupil investment and community support are unmatched in the region.
parksAttractions: Oakwood has 8 beautifully maintained parks including Orchard Park, Shafor Park, and the Tree Farm park area. The entire city is walkable — trails connect neighborhoods, schools, and parks throughout the community. Nearby Patterson Park (adjacent to Oakwood) provides additional recreation space. Oakwood's Smith Park hosts community events and is a neighborhood gathering spot.
housingMarket: Median price $350,000 with Oakwood being one of the most stable real estate markets in all of Ohio — homes hold value even in downturns. Architecture ranges from 1920s Tudor and Colonial Revival to mid-century modern. Inventory is very low and desirable properties often sell within days. Oakwood is considered a 'forever home' market where residents tend to stay long-term.
funFacts: Oakwood has more trees per capita than almost any city in Ohio — its canopy is legendary and fiercely protected by city ordinance. The city has no commercial strips; all retail is thoughtfully tucked away to preserve the residential character. Notable Oakwood alumni include Erma Bombeck, the beloved American humorist and newspaper columnist known for her wit about suburban life.

name: Xenia
tagline: Bicycle Capital of the Midwest — affordable with incredible trail access
overview: Xenia is the county seat of Greene County — a resilient community 15 miles east of Dayton known for its remarkable comeback from devastating tornadoes and its incredible trail system. Called the 'Bicycle Capital of the Midwest,' Xenia sits at the hub of a 300-mile+ interconnected rail trail network where multiple major trails converge. A revitalizing downtown and affordable prices make it a strong value for buyers.
schoolsDetail: Xenia Community Schools is a B- rated district serving approximately 5,000 students. Multiple elementary schools feed into Xenia High School. The district participates in the Miami Valley Career Technology Center (MVCTC) for vocational programs, offering students pathways in trades, healthcare, and technology.
parksAttractions: Xenia is the trail hub of southwest Ohio — the Little Miami Scenic Trail, Prairie Grass Trail, and Creekside Trail all converge here in one of the nation's premier rail-trail networks totaling 300+ miles. Caesar Creek State Park is a short drive away with a lake, hiking, and camping. Downtown Xenia has a charming central square that's been revitalized with local shops and restaurants.
housingMarket: Median home price around $175,000, making Xenia the most affordable option in Greene County. Mix of older ranch and Cape Cod homes in established neighborhoods, with some newer construction on the city's edges. Investors have been active as appreciation accelerates alongside the broader metro's growth.
funFacts: Xenia has been officially recognized as the 'Bicycle Capital of the Midwest' due to its position at the convergence of 300+ miles of rail trails. The city was nearly destroyed by an F5 tornado in 1974 (one of the deadliest in Ohio history) and hit again by a significant tornado in 2000 — its story of resilience and rebuilding is remarkable and has shaped the community's character.

name: Springboro
tagline: One of Ohio's top school districts in a fast-growing suburb
schools: Springboro Community City Schools — A- rating (5-star Ohio)
overview: Springboro is the Dayton area's fastest-growing suburb — up 5% over 5 years — thanks to exceptional schools, high household incomes, and a thriving community along the I-75 corridor. With median incomes of $112,872 and one of the state's top school districts, it attracts corporate relocators and professional families. The historic downtown district sits alongside newer development that continues to push the city forward.
schoolsDetail: Springboro Community City Schools holds one of Ohio's highest ratings — A- overall with a 5-star state rating. 6 schools including Springboro High School, consistently ranked in the top 10% of Ohio public schools. Strong AP programs, award-winning arts, and championship athletics define the Springboro education experience.
parksAttractions: Heritage Park and Miller Park anchor the city's park system, with 400 acres of maintained green space and trails. Springboro's trail system connects neighborhoods to parks and commercial areas. The city is a short drive from Caesar Creek State Park and the Little Miami Scenic Trail. A lively downtown with restaurants, boutiques, and community events rounds out the lifestyle.
housingMarket: Median price $330,000 and appreciating steadily. Strong new construction market with subdivisions attracting young families. Inventory is extremely tight — homes go under contract within days in competitive situations. The high owner-occupancy rate of 86% reflects a committed, stable community of homeowners. Premium pricing reflects the demand for the school district.
funFacts: Springboro sits on a historic Underground Railroad route — several homes in the city's historic district were documented stations helping freedom-seekers reach freedom. The historic district along S. Main Street dates to the 1800s and is listed on the National Register of Historic Places. Today's Springboro is one of the fastest-growing communities in all of southwest Ohio.

name: Bellbrook
tagline: Small-town charm with A-rated schools near Sugarcreek's nature
overview: Bellbrook is one of the Dayton region's best-kept secrets — a small city of 7,200 in Greene County nestled between Beavercreek and the stunning Sugarcreek MetroPark. Known for excellent A-rated schools, extremely low crime, and a quiet small-town feel, it consistently ranks among the safest and most desirable communities in southwest Ohio. Families who find Bellbrook rarely leave.
schoolsDetail: Bellbrook-Sugarcreek Local Schools earns an A rating. Bellbrook High School is consistently among the top schools in Greene County for academics, test scores, and college readiness. Small class sizes and a tight-knit school community create an environment where students are well-known and supported.
parksAttractions: The Sugarcreek MetroPark is Bellbrook's crown jewel — 1,200 acres of stunning natural scenery with hiking trails, a covered bridge, ravines, and wooded valleys. John Bryan State Park in adjacent Yellow Springs extends the hiking and outdoor recreation for miles. The community has a charming village center with local shops and a genuine small-town atmosphere.
housingMarket: Median price around $320,000 — premium pricing reflects the strong schools and low crime. Mostly newer construction from the 1980s–2000s on spacious lots. Very low turnover market; residents tend to stay for years, creating limited inventory. Buyers who commit to Bellbrook consistently report high satisfaction.
funFacts: Much of Bellbrook's surrounding Sugarcreek Township has chosen to remain unincorporated, meaning many residents in the Bellbrook area just outside city limits pay no municipal income tax. The 1,200-acre Sugarcreek MetroPark features one of Ohio's most scenic covered bridge crossings and is especially stunning in fall foliage season.

name: Miamisburg
tagline: Riverside historic 'Burg' with ancient mounds and modern conveniences
highlights: Miamisburg Mound — second largest in North America
overview: Miamisburg is a historic city along the Great Miami River established in 1818 — affectionately called 'The Burg' by locals. Located 15 miles southwest of Dayton with excellent I-75 access, it blends rich history (ancient Native American mounds, an atomic energy past) with modern conveniences like the Austin Landing development. A budget-friendly alternative to Centerville and Springboro with growing appeal.
schoolsDetail: Miamisburg City Schools holds a B rating with 11 schools. Miamisburg High School (the Vikings) is known for an award-winning show choir and marching band, plus competitive athletics including state appearances in baseball and bowling. A new middle school has been built and the high school has seen recent renovations. Participation in the MVCTC provides strong vocational pathways.
parksAttractions: The Miamisburg Mound — one of the two largest conical burial mounds in North America at 65 feet tall — is the city's landmark attraction with 116 steps to a panoramic summit view. Riverfront Park hosts free summer concerts along the Great Miami River. The Great Miami River Trail runs through the city for biking north to Dayton or south to Middletown. Star City Brewing and a walkable downtown round out the local scene.
housingMarket: Median price $250,000 with healthy appreciation (~25% cumulative over 5 years). Downtown and older neighborhoods offer charming historic homes in the $150K–$250K range; newer west-end subdivisions go $300K+. Austin Landing nearby has spurred demand and luxury apartment development. An excellent value alternative to Centerville and Springboro for first-time buyers.
funFacts: Miamisburg was home to the Mound Laboratories — a secret U.S. atomic energy facility and part of the Manhattan Project that developed nuclear triggers. The site near Mound Park is now a business park with a small museum commemorating its significant Cold War history. The Miamisburg Mound is approximately 2,000 years old, built by the Adena people, and stands 65 feet tall with a base diameter of 877 feet.

name: Trotwood
tagline: Most affordable entry point in the Dayton metro
overview: Trotwood is a northwest Dayton suburb offering the most affordable entry-level prices in the metro area. The community is on an improvement trajectory with new investment coming in, though it faces challenges with school ratings and crime compared to other suburbs. Ideal for investors and first-time buyers with a long-term horizon who want maximum square footage per dollar.
schoolsDetail: Trotwood-Madison City Schools holds a C rating. The district has faced challenges but has been investing in facility improvements and academic intervention programs. Students can access the Miami Valley Career Technology Center for vocational training in skilled trades, healthcare, and other fields.
parksAttractions: Trotwood has 100+ acres of parkland including Trotwood-Madison City Park. The community is within easy reach of the broader Dayton park system including the Twin Creek MetroPark. Salem Mall area provides commercial amenities, and I-70 access opens up the Dayton-Columbus corridor.
housingMarket: The most affordable median prices in the Dayton metro at around $95,000. High percentage of rental properties in the market. Investors have been active as the broader metro's appreciation spills into more affordable areas. Buyers looking for maximum space per dollar or investment properties will find the best prices here.
funFacts: Trotwood has one of the largest African American communities in the Dayton metro area. The city is named for William Trotter, one of the early settlers. Several historic churches in Trotwood have deep roots in Ohio's civil rights movement and the Underground Railroad network of the 1800s.

name: Riverside
tagline: Borders WPAFB — the hidden gem for military families
schools: Mad River City Schools — B rating
highlights: Shares a border with WPAFB Area B
National Air Force Museum minutes away
overview: Riverside is a city of 23,000 that directly borders WPAFB to the north and east — making it one of the shortest commutes to the base of any community in the region. Sometimes overlooked in favor of Fairborn or Beavercreek, it offers strong WPAFB proximity at very competitive prices. The National Museum of the U.S. Air Force is literally on the base right at the Riverside border.
schoolsDetail: Mad River City Schools serves Riverside with a B rating across 6 schools. The district serves a diverse community with a high proportion of military families and has invested in facilities improvements. Extracurricular programs include JROTC, reflecting the community's military connection.
parksAttractions: The National Museum of the U.S. Air Force — the world's largest aviation museum with 360+ aircraft — is located on WPAFB at the Riverside border and is free to visit. Mad River trail corridors provide recreational access, and the city has 200+ acres of parkland. The proximity to WPAFB means access to base recreational facilities for military families.
housingMarket: Median price around $175,000 — very competitive given the WPAFB proximity. Mix of post-WWII era homes and some newer construction. Popular with E-5 through O-3 pay grades looking to maximize BAH dollars. Strong military tenant demand keeps the rental market active as well.
funFacts: The National Museum of the U.S. Air Force — the world's oldest and largest aviation museum with over 360 aircraft and missiles on display — is located right on WPAFB at Riverside's border. It's free, open to the public year-round, and one of the most visited attractions in all of Ohio. The Wright Brothers conducted early flight experiments at what is now WPAFB, just steps from Riverside.

name: Yellow Springs
tagline: Artsy, walkable village — unlike anywhere else in Ohio
schools: Yellow Springs Exempted Village Schools — B+ rating
overview: Yellow Springs is unlike any other community in Ohio — an artsy, progressive village of 3,700 with a one-of-a-kind downtown, Antioch College, and the stunning 1,000-acre Glen Helen Nature Preserve. Known for its independent spirit, farm-to-table dining, and quirky character, it draws artists, academics, free thinkers, and people who want to live somewhere truly unique. Dave Chappelle chose it as his home for a reason.
schoolsDetail: Yellow Springs Exempted Village Schools is a small B+ rated district. The community's deep connection to Antioch College creates a unique academic culture emphasizing critical thinking and progressive education. With small class sizes and an engaged community, students receive exceptional individual attention in an environment unlike any other district in the region.
parksAttractions: Glen Helen Nature Preserve is the jewel of Yellow Springs — 1,000+ acres of old-growth forest, ravines, waterfalls, and the famous Yellow Spring itself. Adjacent John Bryan State Park extends the trail network for miles through the Little Miami gorge. Downtown Yellow Springs has thriving independent shops, galleries, restaurants, and farmers markets that draw visitors from across the region.
housingMarket: Median price around $290,000 — elevated relative to other communities of this size due to the intense demand for the unique village lifestyle. Homes range from 19th-century craftsman cottages to mid-century moderns. Very limited inventory as residents hold their properties long-term. This is a lifestyle purchase as much as a real estate decision.
funFacts: Comedian Dave Chappelle famously returned to Yellow Springs from Los Angeles and New York because he loves the community — and he's regularly seen around town. The village has repeatedly resisted commercial development chains to preserve its independent character. Antioch College, one of America's most experimental liberal arts colleges, has been a center of intellectual life in Yellow Springs since 1852.

name: Englewood
tagline: Underrated suburb with strong schools and a massive MetroPark
overview: Englewood is a growing north-of-Dayton suburb of 13,500 that consistently punches above its weight — offering B+ rated Northmont schools, the massive Englewood MetroPark on its doorstep, and affordable prices that undercut comparable communities. With easy I-70 access and a welcoming community feel, Englewood has been quietly drawing families who do their homework.
schoolsDetail: Northmont City Schools earns a B+ rating with 5 schools. Northmont High School is known for strong academics, championship athletics programs (especially football and track), and a supportive community culture that produces high graduation rates and college attendance. Dual enrollment options with nearby universities are available.
parksAttractions: Englewood MetroPark is a regional gem — 1,000+ acres of reservoirs, forests, hiking and biking trails, fishing, and picnic areas along the Stillwater River. It's one of the best outdoor recreation destinations in the Dayton metro, practically in Englewood's backyard. The Stillwater River valley makes this one of the greener, more scenic areas north of Dayton.
housingMarket: Median price around $220,000 with good appreciation in recent years as buyers discover the value here. Mix of 1970s–1990s subdivisions and some newer construction. Buyers get significantly more space and lot size here compared to southern suburbs at similar price points. A strong buy-and-hold market.
funFacts: Englewood sits at the edge of the Stillwater River valley, giving it some of the most scenic natural scenery of any north-Dayton suburb. The Englewood MetroPark reservoir is popular for fishing, waterfowl hunting, and wildlife watching year-round. Northmont High School's athletic programs have produced numerous state championships across multiple sports.

name: Vandalia
tagline: Airport-adjacent suburb with strong schools and easy commutes
highlights: Host to world's largest trapshooting event
overview: Vandalia is a well-regarded suburb north of Dayton, home to the Dayton International Airport. With a stable population of 15,300, strong B+ rated schools, and a solid median income, it's popular with professionals who travel frequently and families seeking a safe, convenient community. Easy I-75 access makes it a natural hub for the north Dayton corridor.
schoolsDetail: Vandalia-Butler City Schools is a B+ rated district with 5 schools. Butler High School has consistently strong graduation rates and offers AP courses and dual enrollment options. The district is known for a supportive community environment and strong extracurricular programs across academics and athletics.
parksAttractions: Vandalia has 454 acres of parkland including Cassel Hills Park, Vandalia Park, and Rohrbaugh Park. The city hosts the Grand American World Trapshooting Championships each summer — the largest clay target shooting competition in the world, drawing 20,000+ participants. The Dayton International Airport is minutes away for frequent travelers.
housingMarket: Median price around $200,000 with stable market conditions. Mix of ranch homes, two-stories, and some newer construction. Areas away from the airport's flight path are quiet and residential. Strong value for buyers who want proximity to both downtown Dayton (13 min) and airport access.
funFacts: Vandalia hosts the Grand American World Trapshooting Championships every summer — the largest clay target shooting competition in the world, drawing over 20,000 competitors from across the U.S. and internationally. The city's name is a nod to 'Vandalia,' a proposed 18th-century American colony. The Dayton International Airport, minutes from Vandalia, is a key regional hub for cargo and passenger travel.

name: Tipp City
tagline: Charming historic downtown with one of Ohio's best school districts
schools: Tipp City Exempted Village Schools — A rating (5-star Ohio)
overview: Tipp City is one of the Dayton area's most beloved small cities — a charming community of 10,100 with a beautifully preserved historic downtown along S. 2nd Street, state-champion school programs, and Great Miami River access. It consistently attracts families who want top schools, community character, and a lifestyle that feels like small-town Ohio at its finest.
schoolsDetail: Tipp City Exempted Village Schools earned a 5-star Ohio state rating — placing it among the very top school districts in the entire state. 6 schools with a small, tight-knit culture. Tipp City High School is known for strong academics, state-champion athletic programs, and a community that shows up for its students. One of the best districts in the Dayton area for academic outcomes.
parksAttractions: 837 acres of parkland including Preservation Park along the Great Miami River, Tipp City Municipal Park, and trails connecting to the Little Miami Scenic Trail network. Downtown Tipp City is a destination in itself — restored brick storefronts, local restaurants, antique shops, and community festivals make it one of the most walkable small-town downtowns in Ohio.
housingMarket: Median home price $300,000 with steady appreciation reflecting strong demand. Homes range from charming in-town Victorian and Craftsman styles to large new construction in neighborhoods like Kingsgate Estates. The combination of low property taxes (1.49%) and top schools makes Tipp City one of the best long-term value propositions in the region.
funFacts: Tipp City's historic downtown is one of the best-preserved in Ohio, recognized as a 'Main Street Ohio' community. The city's annual Mum Festival celebrates chrysanthemums each fall, drawing thousands with street vendors, displays, and community events — a tradition that dates back decades. Tipp City's incredibly low property tax rate of 1.49% is among the lowest of any quality suburb in the region.

name: West Carrollton
tagline: Affordable riverside living with a revitalizing downtown
schools: West Carrollton City Schools — C+ rating
overview: West Carrollton is a small city of 13,000 along the Great Miami River, just south of Dayton adjacent to Miamisburg. Its revitalizing downtown, river trail access, and proximity to Kettering and Miamisburg amenities make it an affordable alternative with growing appeal. The city has been investing in its Main Street corridor and riverfront as part of a broader renaissance.
schoolsDetail: West Carrollton City Schools holds a C+ rating with 5 schools. West Carrollton High School participates in the Miami Valley Career Technology Center for vocational training in skilled trades, healthcare, culinary arts, and other in-demand fields. The district has been working on academic improvement initiatives.
parksAttractions: Access to the Great Miami River Trail for biking and walking is a major draw — you can ride north to Dayton or south toward Miamisburg and beyond. Riverfront Park provides scenic river access for kayaking, fishing, and trail use. The city has been investing in river access improvements and developing its 160 acres of parkland.
housingMarket: Median price around $155,000 — one of the more affordable options in the south Dayton area. Proximity to Miamisburg and Kettering means excellent access to amenities without paying those communities' prices. Downtown revitalization efforts are gradually increasing interest and driving appreciation.
funFacts: West Carrollton has one of the most active kayaking communities in the Dayton region — the Great Miami River here is especially scenic and the city has been investing in put-in and take-out points. The city's Main Street district is undergoing a revival with new restaurants and businesses opening in restored historic storefronts, making it one of the metro's emerging neighborhoods to watch.
